message:
fix(fnp-iter): match numpy.nditer external_loop chunk semantics exactly

The fnp-iter NditerPlan and the embedded Python conformance bridge
were synthesizing external_loop chunk boundaries from a guess
("inner_axis = last for C, first for F; chunk = shape[inner_axis]")
rather than asking numpy what the actual chunks would be. For
trivial contiguous shapes that guess matched numpy, but for any
case where numpy's external_loop coalesces beyond a single inner
axis (the common contiguous-operand path), the rust plan would
emit too many short chunks and the python bridge would emit even
more, so parity tests passed by luck on simple shapes and would
fail silently on real workloads.

Replace the synthesized chunks with what numpy.nditer actually
emits.

Rust side (crates/fnp-iter/src/lib.rs):
- plan_external_loop now takes element_count (not just shape +
  options) so it can use that as the upper bound on a fully
  collapsed inner loop, matching numpy's "if the operand is
  contiguous in the requested order, the entire flat range is one
  chunk" rule.
- The plan emits one collapsed chunk for the contiguous case and
  reverts to per-axis chunks only when the iteration order forces
  a stride break.

Python bridge (the embedded conformance script in fnp-iter):
- Replace the manual `for iterindex in range(start, element_count,
  inner_loop_len)` loop with a real numpy.nditer call:
  - external_loop=True: drive `np.nditer(values, flags=["external_loop"], order=order)`
    and read the chunk boundaries directly off each yielded view.
    The script reads each chunk's first/last linear index, decides
    whether to skip (entire chunk before start) or to raise
    "multi-index must align to external_loop chunk boundaries"
    (start lands mid-chunk).
  - external_loop=False: drive `np.nditer(values, flags=["multi_index"], order=order)`
    and walk one element at a time, taking multi_index off the
    iterator object directly.
- element_count == 0 short-circuits to states=[] before constructing
  the values array — np.arange(0).reshape(()) is fine but
  np.arange(0).reshape(empty_shape) raises in some numpy versions.
- multi_index for shape==[] (zero-d) stays [] in both branches,
  matching the rust plan output.

Conformance tests (crates/fnp-conformance/tests/smoke.rs) renamed
and adjusted to reflect the new semantics:
- nditer_plan_external_loop_c_order_tracks_last_axis →
  nditer_plan_external_loop_c_order_collapses_contiguous_operand
- nditer_plan_external_loop_f_order_tracks_first_axis →
  nditer_plan_external_loop_f_order_collapses_contiguous_operand
- nditer_wrapper_external_loop_groups_last_axis_for_c_order →
  nditer_wrapper_external_loop_emits_one_contiguous_c_order_chunk
- nditer_wrapper_external_loop_f_order_seeks_on_chunk_boundaries →
  nditer_wrapper_external_loop_f_order_rejects_mid_chunk_seek

The bridge-vs-rust f_order seek test
(nditer_python_bridge_matches_rust_f_order_seek_from_iterindex →
nditer_python_bridge_matches_rust_f_order_external_loop_chunk)
drops the explicit set_iterindex(2) on the rust iter and the
matching steps_from_iterindex(2) on the bridge, since both sides
now agree on the natural chunk boundary without manual seeking
into a non-aligned position.

Existing parity for non-external-loop iteration is preserved
end-to-end (the multi_index branch in the python script).

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
